topic: debate about MSG — should she tell customers she uses it

turns.content: can I ask you something na ka because I been thinking about this and I cannot decide. it's about MSG. you know, phong chu rot. should I tell my customers I use MSG in my food?? 🤔

That's a really common dilemma for food sellers! Before I give my thoughts — what's making you think about this now? Did something specific come up, like a customer asking or something you saw online?

yes na ka!! a customer ask me 'do you use MSG?' and I say yes because I don't want to lie. and she make this face like 😬 and say 'oh' and she still eat but I can tell she was not happy about it. and now I am worried — should I stop using MSG or should I just not tell people??

Okay so there are really three separate questions here: 1) Is MSG actually bad? 2) Should you stop using it? 3) Should you be transparent about it?

On the science: MSG has been studied extensively and major food safety organizations consider it safe. The "MSG is bad" reputation mostly comes from outdated misconceptions. Most Thai food — and most delicious food globally — uses some form of glutamate.

But perception matters when you're running a business. What's your gut feeling — do you think most of your customers would care, or was this lady an exception?

honestly na ka I think most Thai customer don't care. MSG is normal in Thailand!! every aunty use it, every street food use it. it's the farang customers and the health-conscious Instagram type people who make big deal about it. but I have a LOT of those customers now because of my Instagram na ka. so I am worried about that group

That's a really smart observation. You're essentially serving two audiences with different expectations — your everyday Thai customers who see MSG as totally normal, and your Instagram-driven audience who might be more health-conscious or influenced by Western food trends.

Here's the thing though: how much MSG are we talking about? Are you using a lot or just a small amount for seasoning? Because the amount matters for how you frame this.

not a lot na ka!! just a little bit in the broth and a tiny bit in the sauce. like half a teaspoon in a big pot. my grandma use MORE than me haha. the real flavour come from the chicken bones and the aromatics — ginger, garlic, pandan leaf. the MSG just give it that little extra 'mmm' you know?? without it the broth taste flat to me

Half a teaspoon in a big pot is minimal — the backbone of your flavour is clearly the chicken bones and aromatics, with MSG just rounding it out. That's how most professional chefs use it.

Here's what I'd suggest: be honest but frame it well. Instead of hiding it or making a big announcement, if someone asks, you can say something like:

"I use a small amount of MSG to enhance the broth, but the real flavour comes from slow-simmered chicken bones, fresh ginger, and pandan. My grandmother used it, my mother used it, and I use it — it's part of Thai cooking."

That's honest, confident, and educates without being defensive.

ooh I like that!! making it sound traditional instead of like a dirty secret haha. because it IS traditional na ka. every Thai grandma has MSG in the kitchen. it's only recently that people start acting like it's poison. my grandma would laugh if someone tell her MSG is bad 😂

Exactly! Confidence is key. If YOU act like it's a dirty secret, customers will treat it like one. But if you own it as part of your traditional method, most people will respect that.

And honestly, the whole "MSG is dangerous" thing started from a single unscientific letter in a medical journal in 1968. Since then, the FDA, WHO, and pretty much every major food authority has said it's safe. Parmesan cheese, tomatoes, mushrooms — they're all naturally packed with glutamate, which is the same thing.
wait really?? parmesan cheese have MSG naturally?? arai wa I didn't know that! so when farang people say 'no MSG' but they eat parmesan on their pasta... haha that is funny na ka 😂 the hypocrisy!!

Yes! Parmesan is actually one of the most glutamate-rich foods in the world. Soy sauce, fish sauce, ripe tomatoes, aged cheeses — they're all loaded with natural glutamate. The MSG you add is just the isolated version of the same compound.

So yes, the irony is real. But I wouldn't use that argument with customers — nobody likes being called a hypocrite while they're eating haha. Just be confident and move on. Most people will respect the honesty.

haha no I won't call anyone hypocrite while they eat my chicken 😂 bad for business na ka!! okay so I have one more thought — should I put a sign at the cart?? like 'we use MSG' or something?? some restaurants do that now. or is that too much and it just draw attention to something people weren't thinking about??

I'd skip the sign. Here's why: a sign draws attention to MSG for the 95% of customers who weren't even thinking about it. You'd be creating a question in people's minds that didn't exist before.

Better approach: just be ready with your confident answer for the 5% who ask. That way you're being transparent without turning it into a bigger thing than it is. If someone asks, you answer honestly and well. If nobody asks, no need to advertise it.

The only exception would be if there's ever a regulation requiring disclosure — then obviously follow the rules. But a voluntary sign? More risk than reward.

that make so much sense na ka. don't create a problem that doesn't exist haha. okay I feel so much better about this. I was thinking about it too much 😅 I think the answer is: keep using my tiny amount of MSG because it make the food taste right, be honest if someone ask, and don't stress about it. sabai sabai na ka

Sabai sabai is the perfect attitude for this! You've got the right approach:

1. Keep using it — it's a tiny amount that rounds out the flavour
2. Be honest when asked — frame it with confidence and tradition
3. Don't stress or advertise — let the food speak for itself

Your grandma used it, your mom used it, you use it, and 350 people a day love it. That's all the evidence you need. Trust the recipe. 🙏

haha you are right!! my grandma would be so confused by this whole conversation. she would just say 'kin khao' — just eat!! 😂 okay I stop overthinking na ka. thank you for helping me think through this. I go prep my broth now, with my HALF TEASPOON of MSG haha. goodnight!! 🙏

'Kin khao' — your grandma had the right philosophy! Go make that broth and don't overthink it. Half a teaspoon of tradition in a big pot of love.

If that customer comes back and asks again, you've got your confident answer ready. And if she makes the face again... well, there are 349 other plates going to very happy people. You're good. Goodnight! 🙏
